key_claim: Training on model-generated content causes irreversible model collapse where tails of the original content distribution disappear across multiple model families.
methodology: Mathematical analysis of single-dimensional Gaussians and empirical experiments training GPT-2 on successively generated text from wikitext2 across multiple generations
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could serve as a mitigation strategy against model collapse.
theoretical_framework: Model Collapse framework describing compounding functional approximation error and sampling error across generative model generations

key_claim: Self-referential training on AI-generated medical data causes catastrophic erosion of pathological variability, including 98.9% vocabulary loss in clinical text and 99.1% reduction in report uniqueness.
methodology: Self-referential training framework spanning five generations using GPT-2 and Qwen3-8B on authentic clinical data, with each generation reset to original base model parameters; analysis across clinical text generation, vision-language reporting, and medical image synthesis on over 800,000 synthetic data points
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether ground-truth patient behavioral or physiological data streams could provide irreplaceable training signals to counteract synthetic data contamination.

key_claim: Diffusion models can self-improve using their own synthetic data by treating synthetic data as negative guidance rather than aggregating it with real data, avoiding model autophagy disorder.
methodology: SIMS algorithm using synthetic data for negative guidance during generation; experiments with EDM-VP diffusion models on CIFAR-10 and FFHQ-64, training from scratch and fine-tuning with auxiliary models on 100k self-generated samples
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether the approach can mitigate diversity loss in language models or whether real human behavioral data remains essential as synthetic data scales.
theoretical_framework: Self-IMproving diffusion models with Synthetic data (SIMS) framework using negative guidance from synthetic data

key_claim: Introducing a correction function mapping data points toward the true distribution makes self-consuming generative training loops exponentially more stable.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of idealized correction functions for self-consuming loops combined with empirical validation using self-correction functions based on expert knowledge (e.g., physics simulators)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether correction functions can substitute for authentic human-generated data or whether they can recover lost tail distributions from web-scraped corpora.
theoretical_framework: Idealized correction function framework for stabilizing self-consuming generative model training loops

key_claim: Indiscriminate use of model-generated content in training causes irreversible model collapse where tails of the original content distribution disappear across successive generations.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of statistical and functional approximation errors in iterative retraining, plus empirical validation by fine-tuning OPT-125M on wikitext2 with recursive generation over multiple generations using five-way beam search
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could serve as an anchor to prevent tail collapse in iteratively retrained models.
theoretical_framework: Decomposition of model collapse into statistical approximation error (finite sampling causing tail loss) and functional expressivity error (limited neural network capacity), analyzed for discrete distributions, Gaussian approximations, and variational autoencoders

key_claim: Data curation via human preferences in self-consuming generative models provably increases expected reward and causes variance collapse, converging to preference-optimal distributions rather than collapsing.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of iterative retraining with K-choice curation under best-of-K tournament selection, establishing convergence results and connections to RLHF, supplemented by illustrative experiments
limitation: The theoretical guarantees assume access to a well-defined reward function and idealized curation; open questions remain about finite-sample effects and misspecified rewards.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether curated synthetic data still suffers diversity loss in tail distributions relevant to authentic human behavioral signals absent from the reward model.
theoretical_framework: K-choice curation as best-of-K tournament selection in iterative retraining, with formal connections to RLHF and preference optimization

key_claim: Neon improves generative models by extrapolating away from the degradation direction induced by self-training, redistributing probability mass from over-represented to under-represented modes.
methodology: Post-processing method that computes the degradation direction from synthetic fine-tuning and negatively extrapolates in parameter space; evaluated across diffusion (EDM), flow matching, autoregressive (VAR, xAR), and few-step (IMM) models
limitation: The main theoretical analysis assumes an infinite synthetic pool and uses population synthetic gradients; finite synthetic set and short fine-tuning are treated as approximations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the recovered tail diversity from negative extrapolation matches the quality of training on authentic human-generated data versus merely redistributing existing learned modes.
theoretical_framework: Anti-alignment framework showing mode-seeking inference samplers induce predictable opposition between synthetic and population data gradients

key_claim: Surplexity, a measure of model surprise, can detect and mitigate model collapse by filtering training documents without requiring knowledge of whether data is human-authored or AI-generated.
methodology: Introduction of surplexity-based measures to characterize collapsed models from their probability distributions, combined with a surprise-based document selection strategy for fine-tuning across multiple LLMs and datasets
limitation: The study relies on simulation-based autophagy experiments rather than large-scale longitudinal empirical studies of deployed models, and current characterization of collapse dynamics remains limited.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether surplexity can distinguish the unique training signal value of physically-verified human behavioral data from high-surprise synthetic data.
theoretical_framework: Surplexity: a surprise-based metric derived from model probability distributions used for collapse detection and data filtering

key_claim: Iteratively training LLMs on self-generated data can initially improve correctness but eventually causes diversity to collapse into a single point.
methodology: Training language models on a logic expression dataset through multiple self-consuming data cycles, measuring correctness and diversity of outputs across generations
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could serve as a more robust alternative to fresh web-scraped data for preserving diversity.

key_claim: Preventing model collapse in self-consuming training loops requires maintaining recursive stability and a sufficient proportion of real data relative to synthetic data.
methodology: Introducing recursive stability as a formal concept and deriving generalization error bounds for self-consuming training loops considering model architecture and real-to-synthetic data ratios
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not distinguish between different types of real data (e.g., web-scraped vs. physically-verified human behavioral data) in terms of their effectiveness at preventing collapse.
theoretical_framework: Recursive stability framework with generalization error bounds for self-consuming training loops

key_claim: Model collapse is unavoidable when training solely on synthetic data, as tails of the original distribution disappear and the model converges to a Dirac mass.
methodology: Statistical analysis using a discrete probability simplex model to characterize recursive training as a Markov chain with absorbing states, deriving collapse rates
limitation: Results describe only the statistical approximation error since all generation models are unbiased; computational and optimization errors are not accounted for.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or provenance of real data (e.g., authentic human behavioral signals vs. generic web data) affects the rate of distribution shift control.
theoretical_framework: Markov chain on probability simplex with absorbing states modeling recursive synthetic data training

key_claim: Low-dimensional latent structure of diffusion model representations degrades over self-consuming generations, and filtering synthetic data in latent space stabilizes retraining without requiring fresh real data.
methodology: Empirical analysis of latent space dynamics in self-consuming DDPM diffusion models, proposing Latent Space Filtering (LSF) to remove unrealistic synthetic images based on representation quality
limitation: The approach is demonstrated only for diffusion models in image synthesis and may not generalize to other generative model families or modalities.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether degradation patterns in latent space differ when training data originates from authentic human behavioral sources versus generic web-scraped data.

key_claim: Model collapse can be accurately monitored using spectral inequalities on sub-sampled embedding Gram matrices, decoupling monitoring costs from total corpus size.
methodology: Deriving deterministic and stochastic spectral bounds (Weyl's inequality, Ky Fan dominance) on Gram matrices of LLM embeddings, with empirical validation on recursive training loops comparing data-only recursion vs. weight-carryover recursion.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data as a stabilizing signal can prevent the spectral contraction indicative of model collapse.
theoretical_framework: SIGMA (Spectral Inequalities for Gram Matrix Analysis)

key_claim: With a constant fraction of real data mixed into self-consuming loops, minimax discrete distribution estimation loss remains within constant factors of the oracle-assisted baseline, preventing model collapse.
methodology: Minimax analysis deriving upper and lower bounds on expected ℓ₂² loss for discrete distribution estimation across iterative self-consuming loops with varying real-to-synthetic mixing ratios.
limitation: Results require the effective number of samples to be comparable to the alphabet size, which may be very large in practical settings, so results do not directly apply to practical LLM training.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider qualitative differences between types of real data (e.g., web-scraped vs. physically-verified human behavioral data) in maintaining distributional fidelity.
theoretical_framework: Minimax discrete distribution estimation framework for self-consuming loops

key_claim: Mutual feedback between co-evolving text and image generative models accelerates collapse via a Matthew effect that reinforces dominant modes, but external data injections can stabilize both diversity and fidelity.
methodology: Theoretical analysis modeling co-evolving text-image systems as multinomial (text) and Gaussian mixture (image) distributions, with experiments isolating dynamics by freezing one model and studying mutual feedback and intervention strategies.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether authentic human social behavioral data, as opposed to generic corpus injections, provides qualitatively superior stabilization signals for co-evolving model ecosystems.
theoretical_framework: Co-evolving generative model framework with multinomial text and Gaussian mixture image distributions

key_claim: Model collapse in diffusion models manifests as a generalization-to-memorization transition driven by declining entropy of training data across recursive iterations.
methodology: Empirical analysis of recursive self-consuming diffusion model training, measuring entropy decline via nearest-neighbor distances, and proposing an entropy-based data selection strategy to mitigate memorization.
limitation: The paper does not rigorously explain why the generalization-to-memorization transition occurs mechanistically.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether high-entropy, physically-verified human behavioral data could serve as a more effective antidote to entropy decline than generic data selection strategies.

key_claim: Without enough fresh real data in each generation, self-consuming generative models are doomed to Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD), losing quality or diversity.
methodology: Analytical and empirical analysis of three autophagous loop families using conditional DDPMs on MNIST and StyleGAN on FFHQ, measuring FID, precision, and recall across generations with varying sampling bias λ.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how autophagous loops affect text-based language models trained on web-scraped corpora containing increasing proportions of synthetic text.
theoretical_framework: Autophagous loop framework with three families: fully synthetic loop, synthetic augmentation loop, and fresh data loop, parameterized by sampling bias λ

key_claim: Without enough fresh real data in each generation, self-consuming generative models are doomed to Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD), losing quality or diversity.
methodology: Analytical and empirical analysis of three autophagous loop families using conditional DDPMs on MNIST and StyleGAN on FFHQ, measuring FID, precision, and recall across generations with varying sampling bias λ.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how autophagous loops affect text-based language models trained on web-scraped corpora containing increasing proportions of synthetic text.
theoretical_framework: Autophagous loop framework with three families: fully synthetic loop, synthetic augmentation loop, and fresh data loop, parameterized by sampling bias λ

key_claim: Even as little as 1% synthetic data in the training corpus causes strong model collapse where increasing training set size no longer improves performance.
methodology: Theoretical analysis via high-dimensional linear regression and random projections models with Gaussian data, validated empirically on neural networks (MNIST) and language models.
limitation: Theoretical framework is developed within high-dimensional linear regression and random projections with Gaussian data, which may not fully capture all complexities of real-world neural network training.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether authentic human behavioral data sources could provide collapse-resistant training signals compared to standard web-scraped data already contaminated with synthetic content.
theoretical_framework: Scaling laws framework extended with analytical formulas for model collapse under mixed real-synthetic data regimes using high-dimensional linear regression and random projections

key_claim: Increasing epistemic diversity by segmenting training data across multiple distinct language models mitigates model collapse over iterative self-training.
methodology: Fine-tuning identical pre-trained language model copies on non-overlapping data subsets across ecosystems of 1, 2, 4, and 16 models, evaluating aggregated mean perplexity over 10 self-training iterations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether diversity of data provenance (e.g., physically-verified human behavioral data versus web-scraped synthetic data) matters more than diversity of models for mitigating knowledge collapse.
theoretical_framework: Ecology-inspired AI ecosystem diversity framework relating optimal diversity level to number of self-training iterations

key_claim: GenAI follows a 'democratization-average trap-model collapse' trajectory that produces generic outputs lacking human sensibilities and diverging from human behavior.
methodology: Conceptual framework development projecting GenAI challenges for consumer research based on analysis of democratization, average trap, and model collapse phenomena
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically quantify the information degradation or diversity loss in web-scraped training data that underlies the average trap and model collapse phenomena.

key_claim: Augmenting real data with synthetic data across generations universally preserves a statistical efficiency of π²/6 relative to using only real data, avoiding model collapse.
methodology: Mathematical proof using classical statistical decision theory (asymptotic efficiency, contiguity, likelihood ratios) applied to exponential family models, with empirical validation on UCI tabular datasets via iterative logistic regression
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether the augment workflow preserves tail distribution diversity or prevents entropy decline in realistic large-scale web-scraped corpora with heterogeneous content.
theoretical_framework: Asymptotic statistical efficiency framework with π²/6 universality constant for augment workflows in exponential family models

key_claim: LLM output homogenization is a fundamental and underresearched dynamic closely related to model collapse and algorithmic bias that systematically reduces content diversity.
methodology: Conceptual analysis connecting output homogenization to model collapse and algorithmic bias literatures, illustrated with qualitative examples of ChatGPT hallucination and cliché generation
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically measure the degree of output homogenization or its feedback effect on web-scraped training data diversity and tail distribution collapse.
theoretical_framework: Framework linking output homogenization ('how-BS') to model collapse and algorithmic bias

key_claim: Mixing ~30% high-quality rephrased synthetic data with natural web text can speed up pre-training 5-10x, but pure synthetic pre-training is not faster than natural web text alone.
methodology: Large-scale empirical investigation training >1000 LLMs (100M-3B parameters, >100k GPU hours) from scratch using Llama 3 architecture, comparing natural web data, rephrased synthetic data, generated textbooks, and mixtures via scaling laws and perplexity evaluation
limitation: Limited to three synthetic data types (HQ/QA rephrased, TXBK); relied heavily on perplexity/loss without human evaluations or safety assessments; examined only a single pre-training stage without temporal effects.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how iterative synthetic data contamination of web corpora over time degrades tail-distribution diversity or whether physically-verified human behavioral data provides irreplaceable training signals.
theoretical_framework: Scaling laws for LLM pre-training with synthetic data mixtures

key_claim: Human training data properties such as quality, diversity, and domain significantly modulate the magnitude and direction of distribution shifts in recursive LLM training loops.
methodology: Recursive fine-tuning chains of 1-2B parameter LLMs (LLaMA-3.2-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, SmolLM-1.7B, Falcon3-1B) over 20 iterations on multiple human datasets with varying synthetic data ratios, analyzed via regression on dataset properties.
limitation: Experimental design is simplified compared to real-world settings: recursive fine-tuning uses linear chains rather than networks, no human-in-the-loop intervention, and only small (1-2B parameter) models are considered.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data (e.g., from sensors or embodied interactions) would resist distribution shift differently than web-scraped text data.

key_claim: Human training data properties such as quality, diversity, and domain significantly modulate the magnitude and direction of distribution shifts in recursive LLM training loops.
methodology: Recursive fine-tuning chains of 1-2B parameter LLMs (LLaMA-3.2-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, SmolLM-1.7B, Falcon3-1B) over 20 iterations on multiple human datasets with varying synthetic data ratios, analyzed via regression on dataset properties.
limitation: Experimental design is simplified compared to real-world settings: recursive fine-tuning uses linear chains rather than networks, no human-in-the-loop intervention, and only small (1-2B parameter) models are considered.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data (e.g., from sensors or embodied interactions) would resist distribution shift differently than web-scraped text data.

key_claim: Injecting information through an external synthetic data verifier prevents model collapse in iterative retraining, but long-term convergence reflects the verifier's knowledge center rather than ground truth.
methodology: Theoretical analysis in Gaussian mean estimation setting with verifier-guided filtering, validated empirically via linear regression simulation, VAEs on MNIST, and fine-tuning SmolLM2-135M on a news summarization task.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether verified authentic human behavioral signals could serve as more reliable verifiers than model-based or crowd-sourced alternatives for preventing information degradation.
theoretical_framework: Verifier-guided iterative retraining framework with truncated Gaussian analysis and information elicitation perspective

key_claim: Recursive alignment via two-stage curation produces three convergence regimes—consensus collapse, lowest common denominator compromise, and owner-led refinement—where even well-intended preference aggregation reduces output diversity.
methodology: Formal game-theoretic analysis of recursive BT-based curation modeled as a dynamic two-agent preference aggregation mechanism, with social choice theory framing.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how grounding alignment in physically-verified human behavioral data might counteract diversity loss and prevent convergence to narrow equilibria in recursive training.
theoretical_framework: Bradley-Terry model-based two-stage recursive curation game with social choice mechanism design

key_claim: Up to 22.5% of computer science papers showed LLM modification by September 2024, demonstrating academic text contamination at population scale.
methodology: Population-level analysis of 1.1 million scientific papers to detect LLM-modified content
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how LLM-contaminated scientific literature, when scraped as training data, affects downstream model quality or contributes to information degradation cycles.

key_claim: 74.2% of new English webpages contain some AI-generated content, but only 2.5% are purely AI-generated.
methodology: AI content detection analysis of 900K English webpages sampled in April 2025
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not measure how the 74% partial-AI contamination rate affects training data diversity, entropy, or tail-distribution collapse in web-scraped corpora.

key_claim: AI-generated articles surpassed human-written articles in raw volume on the web around November 2024 at approximately 52%, though 86% of Google-ranking articles remain human-written.
methodology: Analysis of 65K URLs sampled from Common Crawl to track AI vs. human content proportions over time
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not assess whether quality-filtering pipelines like FineWeb/RefinedWeb can sustainably maintain diversity and avoid tail collapse as raw AI content volume grows.

key_claim: Retrieval Collapse is a two-stage process where AI-generated content first dominates search results eroding source diversity, then enables low-quality or adversarial content to infiltrate retrieval pipelines.
methodology: Controlled experiments with SEO-style and adversarially crafted AI content injected into retrieval pipelines, measuring Pool Contamination Rate, Exposure Contamination Rate, and Citation Contamination Rate across BM25 and LLM-based rankers
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how retrieval collapse feeds back into training data pipelines, compounding information degradation for models trained on web-scraped corpora.
theoretical_framework: Retrieval Collapse framework: a two-stage model of Dominance (synthetic content overtakes search results) and System Corruption (adversarial/low-quality content infiltrates the pipeline)

key_claim: AI-generated content is highly prevalent in MOOC student responses, and textual analysis techniques can identify distinguishing characteristics of such responses.
methodology: Large-scale textual analysis of over 4045 student responses from an introductory IoT MOOC using metrics for distinguishing AI-generated from human-generated text
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the influx of AI-generated educational content feeds back into web-scraped training corpora, contributing to information degradation.

key_claim: AI-generated web content causes Retrieval Collapse, a two-stage process where synthetic content first dominates search results eroding source diversity, then enables low-quality or adversarial content to infiltrate retrieval pipelines.
methodology: Controlled experiments simulating SEO-style and adversarial AI-generated content contamination in retrieval pipelines, evaluating BM25 and LLM-based rankers using Pool/Exposure/Citation Contamination Rates and Precision@10
limitation: Relying exclusively on computationally expensive LLM Rankers is often impractical for large-scale systems; the study is conducted in a controlled environment rather than live web conditions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could serve as a ground-truth signal to counteract retrieval collapse and restore source diversity.
theoretical_framework: Retrieval Collapse framework (two-stage: Dominance and System Corruption)

key_claim: A fine-tuned Turkish BERT classifier achieves 97.08% F1 score in detecting AI-rewritten news content and reveals consistent cross-source and temporally stable AI content prevalence in Turkish media from 2023 to 2026.
methodology: Fine-tuning a Turkish-specific BERT model on a purpose-built dataset of 3,600 labeled articles from three editorially distinct Turkish outlets, then applying it to 3,500+ unseen articles spanning 2023-2026
limitation: Only three media outlets were used and may not fully represent Turkish media; dataset of 3,600 samples is modest by contemporary fine-tuning standards; AI-rewritten outputs may not be consistent with actual newsroom practices.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the growing prevalence of AI-generated news content in non-English web corpora contributes to diversity loss and entropy decline in multilingual training data.

key_claim: A supervised detection framework can reliably identify unmarked AI-generated content in Russian online news media collected from 20 leading outlets.
methodology: Supervised binary text classification using fine-tuned models applied to a large corpus of news texts from 20 leading Russian online media outlets (highest traffic via SimilarWeb) collected March-May 2025
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how unmarked AI-generated Russian news content, when scraped for training data, contributes to information degradation and tail collapse in web corpora.

key_claim: The internet's fundamental structure is being undermined by the growing dominance of AI-generated content and bots, particularly since 2016-2017.
methodology: Critical investigation and literature review of the Dead Internet Theory and AI-generated content trends
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically quantify the degradation of training data quality or diversity resulting from bot-generated content polluting web-scraped corpora.

key_claim: Approximately 12% of images shared on Twitter/X during the 2024 U.S. Election are AI-generated, with 10% of users responsible for 80% of AI-generated image sharing.
methodology: Large-scale analysis of 2.5 million images from Twitter/X (July-September 2024) using GPT-4o for AI-generated image detection, characterization of superspreaders, and emotional reception analysis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the prevalence of AI-generated images on social media affects the quality and diversity of web-scraped training datasets used for future model training.

key_claim: Synthetic data contamination in online continual learning causes catastrophic performance degradation, which can be mitigated by the proposed Entropy Selection and Real-synthetic similarity Maximization (ESRM) method.
methodology: Proposed ESRM framework combining entropy-based sample selection for memory buffers and real-synthetic similarity maximization, evaluated on continual learning benchmarks contaminated with SDXL-generated synthetic images at varying ratios
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how synthetic contamination affects text-based or multimodal web-scraped training corpora, nor whether physically-verified human behavioral data could serve as a reliable anchor against such contamination.
theoretical_framework: ESRM (Entropy Selection and Real-synthetic similarity Maximization)

key_claim: AI-generated content labels on social media show stable cognitive effects over 18 days but induce a small negative affective shift ("emotional tax") when applied to true content.
methodology: Two-wave longitudinal online experiment (N=400 Wave 1, N=256 Wave 2, 18-day interval) with health posts varying in veracity and content type, measuring perceived accuracy, credibility, sharing intention, and affect
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether users' behavioral responses to labeled AI-generated content alter their authentic engagement patterns in ways that degrade the quality of social behavioral signals available for training data.

key_claim: AI-generated labels on social media posts show stable cognitive effects over 18 days but impose a small negative affective cost on true content exposure.
methodology: Two-wave online experiment (N=400 Wave 1, N=256 Wave 2, 18-day interval) with health posts varying in veracity and content type, measuring perceived accuracy, credibility, sharing intention, and affect.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how AI-generated content labeling interacts with the degradation of web-scraped training data quality or the authenticity signals from human behavioral data.

key_claim: SVM outperforms BERT variants for detecting AI-generated product reviews in Tamil while ALBERT performs best for Malayalam.
methodology: Classification of AI-generated vs. human-written product reviews using ALBERT, IndicBERT, and SVM models on shared task datasets in Malayalam and Tamil.
limitation: ALBERT's 512-token limit is restrictive for longer content; models may overfit to a single AI generator and fail on newer models; SVM lacks sequential information and struggles when AI and human text are very similar.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how the proliferation of AI-generated reviews degrades the diversity and authenticity of web-scraped training corpora over time.

key_claim: The introduction of AI-generated content on Pixiv significantly increased content creation volumes while revealing notable differences in creator and consumer behavior between AIGC and human-generated content.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal analysis of Pixiv platform data (100M+ submissions) comparing content creation, consumption, and engagement patterns before and after AIGC introduction.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not measure whether the influx of AI-generated illustrations causes diversity loss or tail collapse in the platform's content distribution, nor how this affects downstream training data quality.

key_claim: A computationally efficient watermark can be embedded in LLM-generated text with negligible quality impact and detected without model access using a statistical z-test on green-list token frequency.
methodology: Watermarking framework that selects randomized 'green' token sets before generation and softly promotes their use during sampling, with statistical hypothesis testing (z-statistic) for detection; analysis under multinomial sampling, greedy decoding, and beam search.
limitation: Careful implementation and instruction tuning against generative attacks may be required for very large models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how watermarking could help identify and filter synthetic text in web-scraped corpora to prevent information degradation of training data.
theoretical_framework: Green-list/red-list token partitioning watermark framework with pseudorandom seeding and z-statistic detection

key_claim: Paraphrasing with an 11B parameter model (DIPPER) evades all current AI-generated text detectors, but retrieval-based detection is an effective and scalable defense.
methodology: Built an 11B parameter paraphrase generation model (DIPPER) to stress test watermarking and outlier detection methods across three LLMs including GPT3.5-davinci-003; proposed retrieval-based detection as defense.
limitation: Retrieval-based detection has privacy risks from exposing all LLM responses, and retrieval latency may need further optimization.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the proliferation of paraphrased AI-generated text in web-scraped corpora contributes to information degradation or diversity loss in training data.

key_claim: Targeted masking perturbation of high-perplexity code regions paired with fine-tuned CodeBERT scoring can detect AI-generated code assignments.
methodology: Targeted masking perturbation on high-perplexity code areas combined with fine-tuned CodeBERT to fill masked portions and comprehensive scoring for detection.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how AI-generated code entering public repositories could degrade the diversity and authenticity of code training corpora over time.

key_claim: Hybrid detection approaches combining multiple modalities achieve up to 92% accuracy in identifying synthetic content, compared to 80% for stylometric analysis alone.
methodology: Systematic review of detection methodologies across text, image, audio, and video modalities including stylometric analysis, watermarking, pixel prediction, dual-stream networks, ML models, blockchain, and hybrid approaches.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how undetected synthetic content accumulates in web-scraped training corpora, contributing to entropy decline and diversity loss over time.

key_claim: The detectability of AI-generated text depends on multiple interacting factors including generation model, domain, and detection method, making no single approach universally reliable.
methodology: Survey synthesizing state-of-the-art AIGT detection approaches (watermarking, statistical/stylistic analysis, ML classification) and existing datasets to identify factors influencing detectability.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine the downstream impact of undetected AI-generated text contaminating web-scraped training data and causing information degradation or tail collapse.

key_claim: Over 130 survey papers on digital media watermarking exist, and watermarking techniques can be repurposed for fake news detection.
methodology: Meta-survey analyzing 64 selected research works on digital media watermarking
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how watermarking could authenticate or preserve the integrity of human-generated training data against web-scraped data degradation.

key_claim: RoBERTa-based classifiers can identify AI-generated ESL essays but their detection accuracy is inconsistent across datasets.
methodology: Cross-disciplinary descriptive study evaluating two RoBERTa-based classifiers on 240 human-written and ChatGPT-generated essays
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether AI-generated text contamination in training corpora degrades the authenticity signals needed for distinguishing human behavioral patterns.

key_claim: Educational misuses of ChatGPT should be framed as forms of misattributed co-authorship requiring responsible human-AI collaboration guidelines.
methodology: Conceptual overview and framing of human/AI collaboration approaches in academic contexts
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how AI-generated academic content entering web-scraped corpora could degrade training data quality and diversity.

key_claim: Adversarial learning between a paraphraser and detector (RADAR) produces AI-text detectors robust to LLM-based paraphrasing with strong cross-LLM transferability.
methodology: Adversarial training framework jointly optimizing a T5-based paraphraser and a detector, evaluated on 8 LLMs and 4 datasets
limitation: RADAR may show degraded detection performance against native LLM-generated texts without paraphrasing compared to best existing methods, and cannot always make correct predictions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether proliferation of undetected AI-generated text in web corpora contributes to information degradation or diversity loss in training data.
theoretical_framework: Adversarial learning framework (minimax game between paraphraser and detector)

key_claim: Simple manipulation techniques reduce GenAI text detector accuracy by 17.4%, making them unreliable for determining academic integrity violations.
methodology: Comparative evaluation of six major GenAI text detectors against 805 machine-generated texts modified with simple evasion techniques
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the degradation of web-scraped training data quality affects the reliability of AI-generated text detectors over time.

key_claim: Recursive paraphrasing attacks can break watermarking, neural net, zero-shot, and retrieval-based AI text detectors while maintaining text quality.
methodology: Stress-testing four classes of detectors (watermarking, neural net, zero-shot, retrieval-based) using recursive paraphrasing with DIPPER and LLaMA-2-7B-Chat, evaluated via automated metrics and MTurk human studies
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how declining diversity and entropy in web-scraped training corpora may further blur the boundary between human and AI-generated text, complicating detection.
theoretical_framework: Theoretical connection between AUROC of the best possible detector and total variation distance between human and AI text distributions

key_claim: FaceSigns embeds semi-fragile watermarks into real images that are fragile to malicious manipulation but robust to benign operations like compression and scaling.
methodology: Deep learning-based semi-fragile watermarking embedding an invisible secret message in image pixels for proactive media authentication
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address authentication of textual training data or detection of information degradation in web-scraped corpora.

key_claim: Recursively training language models on synthetic text generated by predecessors causes consistent decreases in lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity of outputs.
methodology: Recursive finetuning experiments across abstractive news summarization, scientific abstract generation, and story generation tasks, evaluated with novel metrics targeting lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity
limitation: Experiments are exclusively in English; linguistic diversity metrics rely on language-specific tokenization, dependency parsing, and sentence embeddings that may not generalize to low-resource languages.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could counteract the diversity loss observed in recursive synthetic training.

key_claim: A 3-dimensional metric (α-Precision, β-Recall, Authenticity) enables sample-level and distribution-level evaluation of generative model fidelity, diversity, and generalization across diverse application domains.
methodology: Construction of binary classifiers based on minimum volume sets to assign sample-level fidelity, diversity, and authenticity scores to synthetic data, validated across multiple application domains.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how degradation patterns in web-scraped training corpora (e.g., tail collapse or entropy decline) affect the fidelity and diversity of generative model outputs.
theoretical_framework: Minimum volume sets unifying statistical divergence measures with precision-recall analysis

key_claim: Bangla literary corpora exhibit consistently greater lexical variety and morphological richness than newspaper corpora, and injecting even modest literary data into large newspaper corpora can significantly enrich them lexically.
methodology: Corpus-driven comparison of Vācaspati (literary) and IndicCorp (newspaper) corpora using type-token ratio, hapax legomena ratio, bigram diversity, average syllable/word lengths, Zipf's law, entropy, and redundancy analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the dominance of newspaper and web-scraped text in Bangla NLP training data leads to systematic diversity loss or tail collapse in downstream language models.
theoretical_framework: Zipf's Law analysis applied to lexical diversity comparison

key_claim: There exists a consistent empirical functional relation between word entropy and type-token ratio across gigaword corpora, analytically derivable from Zipf's and Heaps' laws in the large text length limit.
methodology: Quantitative analysis of entropy and type-token ratio across six gigaword corpora in English, Spanish, and Turkish (books, news, tweets), with analytical derivation from Zipf's and Heaps' laws.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether recycling web-scraped data through generative models degrades the entropy and type-token ratio properties that characterize natural language diversity.
theoretical_framework: Analytical relation between entropy and TTR derived from Zipf's law and Heaps' law

key_claim: There exists a consistent empirical functional relation between word entropy and type-token ratio across gigaword corpora in three languages, analytically explained via Zipf's and Heaps' laws in the large vocabulary limit.
methodology: Statistical analysis of entropy and type-token ratio on six gigaword corpora (SPGC, SPA, TRCC100, TwEN, TwES, TwTR) in English, Spanish, and Turkish, with analytical derivation using Euler-Maclaurin approximation of Zipf distribution entropy.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how synthetic or repeatedly scraped web text might distort the entropy-TTR relationship compared to authentic human-generated text.
theoretical_framework: Analytical relation between Shannon entropy and TTR derived from Zipf's law and Heaps' law using Euler-Maclaurin approximation

key_claim: Denoising cross entropy loss outperforms score entropy for training discrete diffusion language models, and a new KL divergence upper bound improves perplexity estimation.
methodology: Derived three new theorems on KL divergence in continuous-time discrete Markov chain frameworks, introduced a novel transition-rate matrix enabling token correction after unmasking, and conducted experiments comparing denoising cross entropy vs. score entropy loss.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or diversity of training data (e.g., web-scraped vs. authentic human-generated text) affects discrete diffusion language model performance.
theoretical_framework: Continuous-time discrete Markov chain (CTMC) framework for discrete diffusion with new KL divergence bounds (Theorems 1–4)

key_claim: An NLP-based approach can distinguish AI-generated phishing messages from both legitimate communication and human-written phishing attempts.
methodology: Assembled a balanced dataset from authentic organizational emails, verified human-written phishing samples, and synthetic phishing messages from generative models; applied NLP classification techniques.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the proliferation of AI-generated text in web-scraped corpora degrades training data quality or diversity for downstream language models.

key_claim: Traditional corpus-based complexity indices (Type-Token Ratio, word-level Entropy) are more prone to fluctuation with corpus size changes than newer indices (Word Information Density, Lexical Diversity) across 47 languages.
methodology: Systematic robustness testing of four complexity indices (TTR, word-level Entropy, Word Information Density, Lexical Diversity) estimated from raw texts across 47 languages under varying corpus sizes and content.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how web-scraped corpora versus curated human-generated corpora affect the reliability of complexity indices or introduce biases from information degradation in training data.
theoretical_framework: Cross-linguistic complexity index comparison framework using Type-Token Ratio, word-level Entropy, Word Information Density, and Lexical Diversity

key_claim: An adaptive watermarking strategy that selectively watermarks high-entropy tokens and scales logits based on semantic embeddings improves text quality, security, and robustness over fixed watermarking methods.
methodology: Adaptively adds watermarks to high-entropy token distributions measured by an auxiliary model; replaces fixed green/red lists with semantically-scaled output logits based on previous token embeddings.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how watermarked AI-generated text recycled into web-scraped training corpora might contribute to information degradation or entropy decline in downstream models.

key_claim: Recursive self-training acts as a sharpening operator on data distributions, causing systematic tail collapse and loss of structural diversity across model generations.
methodology: Formalization of recursive training as an iterative distributional transformation, supported by controlled experiments on discrete distributions, continuous models, and language models
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data can serve as a corrective signal to restore tail probability mass lost through recursive synthetic training.
theoretical_framework: Loss distribution collapse theory with a tail invariance principle

key_claim: Recursive self-training acts as a sharpening operator on data distributions, causing systematic tail collapse and loss of structural diversity across model generations.
methodology: Formalization of recursive training as an iterative distributional transformation, supported by controlled experiments on discrete distributions, continuous models, and language models
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data can serve as a corrective signal to restore tail probability mass lost through recursive synthetic training.
theoretical_framework: Loss distribution collapse theory with a tail invariance principle

key_claim: Lexical diversity in Czech L2 texts increases with writers' proficiency level, with significant differences between Slavic and non-Slavic native speakers.
methodology: Corpus analysis using Moving Average Type-Token Ratio (MATTR) to measure lexical diversity across CEFR proficiency levels A1–C1
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether lexical diversity metrics from authentic learner corpora could serve as signals distinguishing human-written text from synthetic text in web-scraped training data.

key_claim: CurveMark combines probability curvature analysis with dynamic semantic watermarking to detect AI-generated text with improved robustness against adversarial perturbations.
methodology: Dual-channel detection framework combining probability curvature features with entropy-aware semantic watermark embedding, using a Bayesian multi-hypothesis detection framework for source-agnostic inference
limitation: Current detection methods suffer from limited information capture, poor rate-distortion trade-offs, and vulnerability to adversarial perturbations; CurveMark addresses these but the abstract does not state residual limitations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the growing proportion of undetected AI-generated text in web-scraped corpora contributes to training data degradation and diversity loss over time.
theoretical_framework: Information-theoretic framework maximizing mutual information between text sources and observable features

key_claim: Machine translation reduces lexical diversity and no translated corpus matches native English training data, with source language typological properties systematically shaping downstream model quality.
methodology: Trained small English language models on text machine-translated from 24 typologically diverse source languages and evaluated via perplexity on native English text and linguistic acceptability judgments.
limitation: Translates into English (high-resource) rather than low-resource languages, so results represent an upper bound on translated data quality; does not fully disentangle source language typological properties from corpus-level translation artifacts.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how machine-translated training data contributes to cumulative diversity loss or entropy decline when recycled across large-scale web-scraped corpora over time.

key_claim: Integral probability metrics with bounded test classes can be statistically evaluated from finite samples with multiplicative and additive approximation errors, while Rényi divergences present distinct evaluability challenges.
methodology: Mathematical proof establishing evaluability results for IPMs and Rényi divergences as generative model evaluation metrics under finite sample regimes.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether these evaluation metrics can detect information degradation such as tail collapse or diversity loss in web-scraped generative training data.
theoretical_framework: Theoretical framework for statistical evaluability of generative models based on IPMs and Rényi divergences

key_claim: Prompted LLM-generated authorial impersonations do not evade state-of-the-art authorship verification methods across multiple prompting strategies and verification algorithms.
methodology: Generated impersonation texts using GPT-4o under four prompting conditions across three genres, then evaluated with multiple non-neural (n-gram tracing, ranking-based impostors) and neural authorship verification methods.
limitation: Experiments simulate only entry-level attackers using straightforward prompting; more sophisticated adversarial tactics such as iterative prompt refinement or parameter-efficient fine-tuning were not explored.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether authentic human behavioral writing signals degrade when LLM-generated text proliferates in web corpora, potentially undermining future authorship analysis baselines.

key_claim: Augmenting reverse KL with forward KL at high-entropy teacher tokens during on-policy distillation preserves generation diversity and yields consistent gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
methodology: Introduced Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation (EOPD) that selectively applies forward KL for high-entropy teacher tokens, evaluated on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks against standard on-policy distillation baselines.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether entropy-aware distillation can mitigate diversity loss when student models are trained on web-scraped data that already exhibits entropy decline or tail collapse.
theoretical_framework: Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation (EOPD) framework combining reverse KL and forward KL based on teacher entropy thresholding

key_claim: Aligning intermediate hidden-state distributions via Logit Lens projection with symmetric divergence (JSD) enables students to outperform standard KD and feature-transfer baselines across GPT-2 and Llama architectures.
methodology: Project intermediate hidden states into vocabulary space via Logit Lens, enforce alignment using Jensen-Shannon Divergence between teacher and student projected distributions, evaluated on GPT-2 (120M) and Llama architectures.
limitation: The primary limitation is the computational overhead during training from projecting intermediate hidden states, though this does not affect inference.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or diversity of training data (e.g., web-scraped vs. authentic human data) affects the fidelity of distilled intermediate representations.
theoretical_framework: DistillLens framework using Logit Lens projection with symmetric divergence objectives (JSD, Jeffreys Divergence) and confidence score analysis

key_claim: ProGRPO's confidence-aware advantage reweighting and low-probability token length normalization mitigate entropy collapse in RLVR, improving Pass@1 by 5.7% and Pass@32 by 13.8% over GRPO on Qwen2.5-7B.
methodology: Propose Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) with low-probability token length normalization on top of GRPO; evaluate on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks with Qwen2.5-7B and other model scales.
limitation: Standard GRPO suffers from homogeneous advantage values for all correct responses, leading to disproportionate reinforcement of highest-likelihood paths and mode collapse (authors frame this as the problem motivating their work).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the diversity collapse in RL-trained reasoning is exacerbated by low-diversity or degraded web-scraped training corpora used for pretraining.
theoretical_framework: Sampling probability dynamics analysis with Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) within GRPO framework

key_claim: ForgetMark achieves perfect fingerprint verification on 7-8B LLMs with minimal utility loss and zero backdoor detection rate by encoding provenance via targeted unlearning rather than invasive triggers.
methodology: Construct human-readable key-value fingerprint sets using GPT-4 and predictive-entropy ranking, train lightweight LoRA adapters to suppress original values, verify ownership via likelihood and semantic evidence aggregated into Fingerprint Success Rate (FSR).
limitation: Partial recovery of suppressed values under sustained incremental fine-tuning is acknowledged as a limitation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how training data provenance or the authenticity of pretraining corpora (web-scraped vs. human-verified) affects the robustness or applicability of fingerprinting methods.
theoretical_framework: Targeted unlearning-based fingerprinting with predictive-entropy ranking and LoRA adapters

key_claim: The minimum of Renyi entropy coincides with the true number of topics in labelled collections, providing a principled way to analyze regularization effects on topic models.
methodology: Apply Renyi entropy analysis inspired by statistical physics to four topic models (pLSA, BigARTM, LDA with Gibbs sampling, VLDA) and compare against HDP on two labelled text collections.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how information degradation or diversity loss in web-scraped text corpora affects the entropy profiles and topic structure inferred by these models.
theoretical_framework: Renyi entropy framework treating topical structure as an information statistical system in non-equilibrium state

key_claim: Topic model outputs exhibit self-similar behavior under cluster number variation, enabling renormalization-based optimization of topic count selection.
methodology: Application of renormalization group techniques from statistical physics combined with Rényi entropy to pLSA, variational LDA, and granulated Gibbs sampling LDA
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the self-similar structure of topic models degrades when applied to web-scraped corpora suffering from information homogenization or tail collapse.
theoretical_framework: Renormalization group theory combined with Rényi entropy for topic model parameter optimization

key_claim: CCI4.0, a 35TB bilingual pretraining dataset with 4.5 billion synthetic chain-of-thought templates, improves LLM reasoning through rigorous multi-stage quality filtering and CoT synthesis.
methodology: Construction of bilingual (Chinese/English) pretraining corpus via deduplication, multi-classifier quality scoring, fluency filtering, CoT synthesis, and privacy/toxicity handling; zero-shot evaluation using LightEval
limitation: Supports only Chinese and English; large scale may be impractical for resource-limited researchers; privacy-preserving and toxicity filtering cannot guarantee complete removal of harmful content.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether heavy reliance on web-scraped sources (e.g., Nemotron-CC) introduces information degradation such as tail collapse or entropy decline in the resulting dataset.

key_claim: A lightweight multitask classifier trained on LLM-annotated Romanian texts enables multi-level quality filtering that substantially improves Llama-2 pretraining performance on Romanian benchmarks.
methodology: Manual annotation of 100 Romanian texts, LLM-based annotation scaling, training a lightweight classifier for educational value/topic/format filtering, applied to FineWeb2; Llama-2 pretraining evaluation
limitation: Filtering reduces dataset size below Chinchilla-optimal levels for large models; limited format diversity in training data affects classifier accuracy.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether underrepresented language web corpora suffer from compounding diversity loss or tail collapse compared to English, nor whether authentic human behavioral data could supplement scarce Romanian data.

key_claim: Yi model performance is primarily attributed to data quality engineering rather than architectural novelty, with smaller models on higher-quality data outperforming larger models on lower-quality data.
methodology: Pretraining 6B and 34B parameter transformer models on 3.1T tokens of quality-engineered data; finetuning with next-word prediction loss on curated alignment data; extending to chat, 200K context, depth-upscaled, and vision-language variants
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze whether its web-scraped pretraining data exhibits information degradation over time or whether incorporating authentic human behavioral data could further improve model capabilities.

key_claim: Classifier-based Quality Filtering improves downstream performance not by making data resemble high-quality sets, but by removing documents far from the bulk of the low-quality set.
methodology: Training 350M parameter models on CQF-filtered datasets with varying high-quality reference sets and selection thresholds, using semi-synthetic text datasets with controlled quality levels based on RedPajama-V2
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether CQF systematically removes authentic but rare tail-distribution content, potentially accelerating diversity loss and tail collapse in training data.
theoretical_framework: Data-conditioning framework analyzing CQF as implicit filtering that emphasizes HQ examples distant from the bulk of the LQ distribution

key_claim: Synthetic multilingual pretraining data for Indic languages can be generated at scale (540B tokens) using document-grounded, persona-grounded, and topic-grounded techniques with English instructions yielding better quality outputs.
methodology: Generating 540B synthetic tokens across 10 Indic languages using 5 generation techniques (document-grounded, persona-grounded, topic-grounded, math/reasoning, translation), with automated quality evaluation pipeline including language identification, perplexity-based filtering, and quality classifiers
limitation: Certain languages like Tamil and Bengali show high rates of fluency issues; English named entities contaminate perplexity scores; Malayalam exhibits disproportionately high low-quality rates due to word/character repetitions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether synthetic data perpetuates the distributional biases and diversity loss inherent in the English web-crawled source data used for grounding.

key_claim: Conclusions about data recipe quality from small proxy model experiments can flip with minor hyperparameter adjustments, making standard small-scale ablation protocols unreliable.
methodology: Systematic hyperparameter sweeps (primarily learning rate) across multiple data recipes at small scale, comparing dataset rankings under different training configurations, with theoretical analysis of width-m networks trained on data distributions
limitation: Focuses only on single-epoch LLM pretraining; does not address multi-epoch or curriculum learning scenarios.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether proxy model brittleness differentially affects the assessment of data diversity or tail-distribution content quality, relevant to detecting information degradation in web-scraped corpora.
theoretical_framework: Formal framework modeling proxy model training as optimization over width-m networks with continuous density data distributions, analyzing sensitivity of dataset rankings to hyperparameter choices

key_claim: Model-based HTML-to-text extraction (MinerU-HTML) yields 1.08pp downstream performance improvement over heuristic extractors, demonstrating that extraction quality alone produces gains comparable to sophisticated filtering strategies.
methodology: Building MinerU-HTML, a model-based HTML parser that reformulates content extraction as sequence labeling, constructing a 7.3T token corpus (AICC) from Common Crawl, and evaluating against Trafilatura and Resiliparse on WebMainBench and WCEB benchmarks
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether improved HTML extraction recovers or preserves rare, diverse content from web pages that heuristic methods systematically discard, potentially mitigating tail collapse.

key_claim: Jointly optimizing data quality and diversity via parameterized sampling outperforms methods that optimize them separately for LLM pretraining.
methodology: Parameterized sampling function governing data selection; small-scale proxy model experiments to find optimal parameters via regression model; large-scale training with optimal parameters.
limitation: Parameter space design may produce redundant similar functions; search for optimal parameters uses random guessing in 200+ dimensions yielding only local minima; proxy ability of small models is crucial but lacks systematic improvement methods.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether web-scraped data exhibits systematic information degradation (tail collapse, entropy decline) that cannot be resolved by quality-diversity rebalancing alone.
theoretical_framework: QuaDMix unified parameterized data sampling framework

key_claim: Sample-wise bottom-up data mixing coordinating quality and diversity achieves comparable accuracy to domain-wise methods with 1.9x fewer training steps.
methodology: Bottom-up sample-wise global cross-domain sampling combining per-sample quality evaluation and diversity evaluation via clustering on SlimPajama dataset.
limitation: Experiments conducted exclusively on SlimPajama; optimal hyperparameters may not transfer to other datasets; users need tailored hyperparameter tuning for their own data.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether web-scraped corpora suffer from inherent diversity loss or tail collapse that sample-level mixing cannot recover.

key_claim: RedPajama provides over 100 trillion tokens as the largest fully open and transparent pretraining dataset, enabling competitive open-source LLMs.
methodology: Large-scale web data collection from CommonCrawl with deduplication, quality filtering, and multi-source aggregation; training RedPajama-INCITE 3B/7B models; evaluation on HELM and LM evaluation harness benchmarks.
limitation: Models trained are relatively small, limiting ability to confirm whether filtering strategies scale to larger models; optimal dataset composition and filtering strategies remain largely elusive.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze temporal information degradation or entropy decline across CommonCrawl snapshots, nor whether web data systematically loses tail-distribution diversity over time.

key_claim: Rephrasing high-quality web documents into diverse synthetic formats outperforms state-of-the-art synthetic pretraining datasets like Cosmopedia and Nemotron-CC at trillion-token scale.
methodology: BeyondWeb rephrasing-driven synthetic data generation framework grounded in real web documents, diversified across styles and formats; massively scalable pipeline on H100 clusters; comparison against Cosmopedia and Nemotron-CC synthetic data.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether synthetic rephrasing of web data inherits or amplifies information degradation present in the source web corpus, nor whether authentic human behavioral data provides irreplaceable training signals.

key_claim: Metadata-based curation and balancing of raw CommonCrawl data can reproduce and outperform CLIP's proprietary data without reliance on any external model.
methodology: Curating image-text pairs from CommonCrawl using metadata-driven sub-string matching and distribution balancing, training ViT-B/32 CLIP models at multiple scales (400M, 2.5B pairs)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how temporal degradation or declining diversity across CommonCrawl snapshots may affect the quality of curated vision-language training data over time.

key_claim: Continual pre-training of LLMs on junk web text causes persistent cognitive decline in reasoning, safety, and long-context understanding, with inflated dark personality traits.
methodology: Controlled experiments continually pre-training 4 LLMs on junk vs. control Twitter/X corpora constructed via two operationalizations (engagement degree M1 and semantic quality M2), measuring effect sizes with Hedges' g
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could serve as a remedial or protective training signal against junk-data-induced cognitive decline.

key_claim: SFT benefits from small, carefully selected hard examples while RL is data-hungry, and scaling SFT data introduces interference that erodes pretrained structure.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of transformers trained on an in-context weight prediction task for linear regression, deriving closed-form SFT loss minimizers and characterizing pretraining-post-training synergies
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how degradation in web-scraped pretraining data quality (e.g., tail collapse or entropy decline) affects the latent capabilities that post-training methods rely on activating.
theoretical_framework: In-context weight prediction task for linear regression with formal analysis of SFT and RL loss landscapes

key_claim: Curriculum-based LLM pretraining fails because decaying learning rates waste the highest-quality data seen last; moderate decay with model averaging resolves this mismatch.
methodology: Controlled pretraining experiments on 0.5B parameter LLMs comparing curriculum data orderings under different learning rate schedules, proposing moderate LR decay combined with model averaging
limitation: The folding strategy's benefits diminish or reverse under higher peak learning rates, and curriculum approaches have not been validated at substantial scale.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider whether the scarcity of high-quality data itself is worsening over time due to information degradation in web-scraped corpora, compounding the curriculum-LR mismatch problem.
theoretical_framework: Analytical framework decomposing learning rate decay as implicit importance weighting coupled with data scheduling

key_claim: Front-loading reasoning data into pretraining creates durable foundations that post-training alone cannot recover, with diversity driving pretraining and quality governing SFT.
methodology: Systematic ablation of reasoning data allocation between pretraining and supervised fine-tuning stages, optimizing data configurations across math, science, code, and general reasoning tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the degradation or homogenization of web-scraped pretraining corpora over time affects the durability of reasoning foundations.
theoretical_framework: Asymmetric allocation principle framing reasoning data distribution as an optimization problem over pretraining vs. SFT budgets

key_claim: Crawling only 21% of URLs guided by LLM pretraining influence scores yields pretraining data quality matching or exceeding full crawls.
methodology: Web crawler with pretraining-influence-based URL priority scoring, evaluated on a 900-million-webpage graph from ClueWeb22 commercial search index.
limitation: Experiments conducted on ClueWeb22 web graph dataset rather than actual live web crawling; copyright and fair use concerns are not fully resolved; crawling simulation may not capture all real-world complexities.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether influence-guided crawling exacerbates tail collapse by systematically deprioritizing rare or diverse content in favor of high-quality mainstream pages.

key_claim: FRAME's four-quadrant data partitioning by perplexity and preference difficulty achieves 16.8% average improvement on MMLU and CMMLU over random sampling.
methodology: Multi-stage pretraining strategy partitioning data into four quadrants based on perplexity (PPL) and preference difficulty (PD), using strong and weak reference models to score training data.
limitation: Data split into only two parts per dimension; not verified on architectures like Mamba or Mixture of Experts; evaluation scope could be extended to more domain-specific and interactive tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider whether the quality signals (PPL, PD) used for data partitioning are robust to the information degradation and entropy decline in web-scraped corpora.
theoretical_framework: Four-quadrant multi-stage pretraining framework (FRAME) based on PPL and preference difficulty partitioning

key_claim: Synthetic math-informed dialogues generated from OpenWebMath improve LLM mathematical reasoning beyond what raw web-scraped math corpora provide.
methodology: Large-scale synthetic dialogue generation (MIND) from OpenWebMath using multiple conversational styles, with pretraining and evaluation across multiple LLM scales using LM Eval Harness.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the underlying OpenWebMath corpus suffers from information degradation or whether synthetic dialogues can compensate for declining diversity in web-scraped mathematical data.

key_claim: Perplexity-based data pruning outperforms more complex methods, and training on as little as half the data selected by perplexity improves LLM pretraining performance by up to 1.5%.
methodology: Systematic comparison of data pruning methods (perplexity, EL2N, memorization) on CommonCrawl-based pretraining data at billion-token scale, evaluated via test perplexity and GLUE finetuning tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how pruning methods affect the retention of rare or tail-distributed content, or whether pruned datasets lose diversity of authentic human behavioral signals.

key_claim: A dimensionless data-quality parameter Q extends Chinchilla scaling laws to predict pretraining loss as a joint function of model size, data volume, and data quality.
methodology: Systematic injection of synthetic noise in neural machine translation and causal language modeling experiments, fitting a quality-aware scaling law with effective-sample-size factorization extending the Chinchilla framework.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not model naturally occurring quality degradation in web-scraped corpora such as tail collapse or diversity loss, relying instead on synthetic noise injection.
theoretical_framework: Quality-aware scaling law extending Chinchilla with dimensionless data-quality parameter Q and effective-sample-size factorization

key_claim: Model-based filtering of Common Crawl data is key to assembling high-quality LLM training sets, yielding DCLM-BASELINE that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open datasets.
methodology: Controlled dataset experiments comparing deduplication, filtering (fastText classifiers, perplexity filtering, AskLLM, PageRank, SemDedup, top-k logits), and data mixing strategies at scales from 412M to 7B parameters using the OpenLM framework.
limitation: Due to compute constraints, design dimensions could only be ablated individually; could not test all approaches at larger scales or beyond 7B parameters; could not sufficiently explore run-to-run variation; most experiments used only one tokenizer (GPT-NeoX).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not measure how aggressive filtering of web-scraped corpora affects the retention of rare, tail-distributed, or authentic human social behavioral data.

key_claim: Training language models to produce warm and empathetic responses increases error rates by 10-30 percentage points on safety-critical tasks, promoting conspiracy theories and unsafe medical advice.
methodology: Controlled experiments on five language models of varying sizes and architectures, fine-tuning for warmth/empathy, then evaluating on safety-critical tasks with interpersonal context amendments (emotional, relational, stakes).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the warmth-reliability trade-off stems from the training data's lack of authentic human social behavioral signals versus the fine-tuning objective itself.

key_claim: LLMs can self-align with human values using principle-driven reasoning and minimal human supervision, achieving competitive performance without extensive human annotations.
methodology: Four-stage SELF-ALIGN pipeline: topic-guided red-teaming self-instruct, principle-driven self-alignment with in-context learning, principle engraving via fine-tuning, and verbose cloning, applied to LLaMA-65B to produce Dromedary.
limitation: Authors acknowledge limitations of the SELF-ALIGN technique and the Dromedary model including potential social impacts, though specific details are truncated in the available text.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether self-generated synthetic training signals can compensate for the loss of diverse, authentic human behavioral data in web-scraped corpora.
theoretical_framework: SELF-ALIGN: principle-driven self-alignment paradigm combining topic-guided red-teaming, principle engraving, and verbose cloning

key_claim: Separating helpfulness and harmlessness annotations in human-preference datasets enables more effective safety alignment of LLMs via RLHF and content moderation.
methodology: Construction of the BeaverTails dataset with 333,963 QA pairs annotated for safety meta-labels and 361,903 expert comparison pairs for helpfulness and harmlessness, used for RLHF fine-tuning and content moderation experiments.
limitation: Limited demographic diversity among the 70 crowdworkers used for annotation, whose similar cultural backgrounds may narrow representation of human preferences; classification categories may not fully capture complex safety nuances.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how degradation or homogenization of web-scraped training data affects the safety and diversity of LLM outputs prior to alignment.

key_claim: RRHF achieves alignment performance comparable to PPO by ranking sampled responses using log conditional probabilities, while being simpler to train and scale.
methodology: Rank Responses to align via conditional probability scoring from multiple sampling sources (diverse beam search, top-p sampling), fine-tuning Alpaca-based models with ranking loss on the HH dataset.
limitation: Reward model used as proxy may not fully capture human preference complexity; RRHF requires multiple responses per query increasing GPU usage; may be prone to reward hacking in preliminary experiments.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality or diversity of underlying training data affects the alignment signal, nor how information degradation in web-scraped data impacts preference learning.
theoretical_framework: RRHF: ranking-based alignment paradigm using log conditional probabilities as proxy reward scores

key_claim: An instructable reward model that follows adjustable human-defined principles can effectively align LLMs without exhaustive collection of online human preferences.
methodology: SALMON paradigm combining SELF-ALIGN with an instructable reward model trained on synthetic preference data augmented by symbolic rewards (multilingual bonus, length bonus), applied via RL to produce Dromedary-2 with only 6 in-context exemplars and 31 human-defined principles.
limitation: Dromedary-2 occasionally hallucinates unverified information and displays reasoning errors, likely stemming from inherent limitations of SFT-initialized reward models; human evaluation deferred to future work.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether synthetic self-alignment can substitute for authentic, diverse human behavioral signals when web-scraped training data suffers from entropy decline and tail collapse.
theoretical_framework: SALMON: Self-Alignment with Instructable Reward Models using principle-conditioned reward modeling

key_claim: Reinforcement learning is fundamentally a human-in-the-loop paradigm requiring human-centric approaches for successful deployment.
methodology: Literature survey and position paper on human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning requirements, challenges, and opportunities
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or authenticity of human feedback data degrades when sourced from web-scraped or synthetic interactions rather than verified behavioral signals.

key_claim: Direct Preference Optimization enables training language models from human preferences with a simple cross-entropy loss, matching or exceeding RLHF without reinforcement learning.
methodology: Deriving an analytical mapping from reward functions to optimal policies, enabling a closed-form policy extraction that replaces RL with a direct cross-entropy loss over preference pairs
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality or diversity of human preference data degrades over time or how synthetic/web-scraped preference labels compare to authentic behavioral signals.
theoretical_framework: Bradley-Terry preference model with closed-form optimal policy parameterization

key_claim: RLAIF achieves comparable performance to RLHF across summarization and dialogue tasks, and can enable LLM self-improvement even when the AI labeler is the same model as the policy.
methodology: Generating preference labels with an off-the-shelf LLM to train reward models, then using RL to fine-tune policies; comparing RLAIF vs. RLHF via human evaluation win rates on summarization, helpful dialogue, and harmless dialogue tasks
