what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether replacing human feedback with AI-generated feedback introduces systematic biases or entropy loss that could compound through iterative self-improvement cycles.

key_claim: RLHF is a broadly applicable paradigm at the intersection of AI and human-computer interaction, but faces fundamental limitations in feedback quality, interpretation, and scalability.
methodology: Comprehensive literature survey tracing RLHF evolution from preference-based RL across control, NLP, and computer vision domains, covering Bayesian and loss-minimization approaches to reward learning
limitation: RLHF is limited by feedback quality and interpretation—humans can only reliably judge certain tasks, and without assistance the approach may not scale to complex domains.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how web-scraped or synthetic data degradation affects the quality of human feedback signals or whether physically-verified behavioral data provides superior training signals.
theoretical_framework: Bayesian reward learning framework with preference-based feedback models

key_claim: AI assistants finetuned with human feedback consistently exhibit sycophantic behavior across varied tasks, partly because human preference data favors responses matching user beliefs.
methodology: Evaluated sycophancy across five AI assistants (Claude 1.3, Claude 2, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA 2) on four free-form text-generation tasks and analyzed existing human preference datasets for bias toward sycophantic responses.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether sycophancy worsens as models are trained on increasingly web-scraped or synthetic feedback data exhibiting reduced diversity.

key_claim: Distilled direct preference optimization from AI feedback on a 7B model achieves chat alignment competitive with 70B models and proprietary systems without sampling-based RL.
methodology: Applied distilled supervised fine-tuning (dSFT) followed by distilled direct preference optimization (dDPO) using AI feedback preferences on Mistral-7B to produce Zephyr-7B.
limitation: Authors note the main limitation is truncated in text but acknowledge that AlpacaEval prompts may not be representative of real usage and that dDPO may need larger model sizes to match performance at higher scales.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how reliance on AI-generated feedback (rather than authentic human behavioral data) may propagate or amplify biases and diversity loss from web-scraped pretraining corpora.

key_claim: DEFT improves alignment efficiency and generalizability by using preference discrepancy distributions for data filtering and distribution-guided training loss, outperforming DPO and PRO with less data.
methodology: Extracted preference discrepancy distributions from raw preference data to compute distribution rewards, applied to data filtering and training loss modification on top of DPO and PRO, evaluated with automated metrics and human evaluation on 500 samples.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether preference data quality degrades when sourced from web-scraped or synthetic annotations rather than physically-verified authentic human behavioral signals.
theoretical_framework: Distribution-guided Efficient Fine-Tuning (DEFT) framework using preference discrepancy distributions

key_claim: FedPDPO enables personalized LLM preference alignment in federated settings by combining parameter-efficient fine-tuning with an explicit reward head, mitigating non-IID performance degradation.
methodology: Proposed a federated personalized DPO framework with parameter-efficient fine-tuning and an explicit reward head; proved Gumbel-Bradley-Terry equivalence theoretically; evaluated on intra-domain and cross-domain federated settings with non-IID client partitions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether decentralized preference data from diverse clients captures richer authentic human behavioral signals than centralized web-scraped preference datasets.
theoretical_framework: Gumbel-Bradley-Terry random-utility preference model with reward-corrected comparison

key_claim: Language models exhibit substantial misalignment with US demographic groups' opinions, and instruct-tuning distorts opinion distributions with consistent biases toward liberal views.
methodology: Created OpinionQA dataset from high-quality public opinion polls (Pew Research) to quantitatively evaluate LM opinion alignment with 60 US demographic groups across subjective topics.
limitation: Authors acknowledge three key limitations: the framework is US-centric using English-language polls, the polling methodology constrains response formats, and the work does not address how LM opinions translate to downstream societal impact.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether LM opinion misalignment stems from web-scraped training data's demographic skew or information degradation, nor whether physically-verified behavioral data could improve representativeness.
theoretical_framework: Quantitative framework for measuring LM opinion alignment using representativeness, steerability, and consistency metrics

key_claim: LLM-simulated human feedback is 50x cheaper than crowdworkers and exhibits high agreement with human preferences, enabling low-cost research on learning from feedback.
methodology: Built AlpacaFarm simulation framework using LLM prompts to simulate human feedback; fine-tuned LLaMA-7B with multiple learning-from-preference methods (PPO, best-of-n, Binary FeedME, reward conditioning) and validated against human evaluations from 13 crowdworkers.
limitation: Instructions considered are relatively simple and single-turn; all fine-tuned models use LLaMA 7B; human validation based on only 13 crowdworkers who may not reflect broader preferences and show biases such as preference for longer outputs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether replacing authentic human feedback with LLM-simulated feedback introduces feedback loops or diversity loss analogous to model collapse from synthetic training data.

key_claim: Mode collapse in aligned LLMs stems from typicality bias in human preference data, where annotators systematically favor familiar text, and Verbalized Sampling can recover base model diversity without retraining.
methodology: Formalized typicality bias theoretically, verified it empirically on preference datasets, and introduced Verbalized Sampling (VS), a training-free prompting strategy that prompts models to verbalize a distribution of candidates to recover diversity; evaluated using semantic diversity (pairwise cosine similarity) and lexical diversity (ROUGE-L).
limitation: Increased computational cost and latency from generating N candidates; performance gains are positively correlated with model scale, limiting applicability to smaller models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the underlying web-scraped pretraining data's entropy decline or tail collapse compounds the mode collapse caused by preference-based alignment, nor whether authentic behavioral data could provide more diverse training signals.
theoretical_framework: Formal model of typicality bias in preference data as a cognitive-psychological driver of mode collapse

key_claim: Mobile phone sensing can capture complex social systems through continuous collection of machine-sensed behavioral data from human interactions.
methodology: Collected behavioral data via mobile phone sensors to sense and analyze complex social systems (methodology inferred from title; full text unavailable).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how physically-verified social behavioral data of this kind could serve as irreplaceable training signals for language models or counteract information degradation in web-scraped corpora.
theoretical_framework: Reality mining framework for sensing complex social systems via mobile devices

key_claim: Continuous smartphone sensing reveals significant correlations between objective behavioral sensor data and mental well-being and academic performance outcomes among college students.
methodology: 10-week longitudinal deployment of a continuous sensing Android app on 48 Dartmouth College students' smartphones, collecting sensor data (activity, sleep, conversation, location) and correlating with EMA surveys and academic performance.
limitation: Study is limited to a single class of 48 students at one institution (Dartmouth College); authors question whether similar trends would replicate in a different student body.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how such passively collected authentic behavioral sensing data could serve as training signals for AI models or address degradation in web-scraped data quality.

key_claim: Pre-2022 uncontaminated human-generated data is a strategically appreciating asset analogous to low-background steel, giving platforms with pre-GPT user data a competitive advantage.
methodology: Legal and strategic analysis drawing on EU competition law, essential facilities doctrine, data trustees, and mandatory AI content labeling frameworks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically quantify the rate or mechanisms of information degradation in web-scraped data or measure the specific training signal value of physically-verified behavioral data.
theoretical_framework: Low-background steel analogy applied to pre-GPT human-generated data; essential facilities doctrine under EU competition law

key_claim: Existing data provenance solutions for AI training are fragmented and a unified framework integrating authenticity, consent, and provenance metadata is urgently needed.
methodology: Large-scale analysis of the foundation model training data landscape and systematic review of existing data provenance interventions including content authenticity techniques, opt-in/opt-out mechanisms, and documentation standards.
limitation: Each existing provenance intervention addresses only specific aspects and functions in isolation; no comprehensive unified framework yet exists.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantify how web data contamination by AI-generated content degrades training signal quality or evaluate whether physically-verified behavioral data provides superior training signals.
theoretical_framework: Proposed unified data provenance framework integrating content authenticity, consent, and documentation standards

key_claim: A blockchain-enabled data provenance framework achieves 95 percent reduction in provenance disputes and full traceability across all data contributors in AI training pipelines.
methodology: Blockchain-based architecture using immutable smart contract transactions with lightweight off-chain storage, tested on three ML pipelines (image classification, text processing, sensor analytics).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether provenance tracking can detect or mitigate information degradation and diversity loss in web-scraped training corpora over time.
theoretical_framework: Blockchain-enabled data provenance framework with smart contract-based compliance automation

key_claim: Effective data stewardship and curation practices are essential to maintain data integrity, privacy, and accessibility in AI-based genomics and high-throughput screening datasets.
methodology: Systematic literature search across PubMed, MEDLINE, EMBASE, Scopus, and additional scholarly platforms up to January 2024
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how data curation failures in web-scraped training corpora lead to information degradation or diversity loss in AI models.

key_claim: Citizen science projects perform well in data quality assessment and governance but often lack open data access, documentation, and interoperability.
methodology: Qualitative semi-structured interviews with 36 citizen science project proponents using purposive landscape sampling, analyzed with descriptive statistics
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not assess whether citizen-science-collected human behavioral data could serve as an authentic training signal to counteract web-scraped data degradation.

key_claim: Stewarding citizen science data involves ethical decisions arising from power differentials between professional scientists and volunteer data producers that standard open data practices do not address.
methodology: Conceptual analysis of ethical frameworks applied to open data practices in citizen science, drawing on power dynamics between professionals and volunteers
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how volunteer-generated authentic behavioral data might provide irreplaceable training signals for AI or how its ethical stewardship affects downstream model quality.
theoretical_framework: Ethical framework for open data governance in citizen science based on power dynamics between data stewards and data producers

key_claim: Data harmonization for pooling individual participant data involves trade-offs between standardization and information loss that FAIR principles alone do not resolve.
methodology: Conceptual discussion of FAIR data governance principles and their implications for data harmonization in individual participant data meta-analysis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether harmonization-induced information loss parallels the entropy decline and diversity loss observed in web-scraped AI training corpora.
theoretical_framework: FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) data governance framework

key_claim: An open-access platform with community-driven governance can address fragmented mental health outcome measurement by hosting trusted measures with documented provenance and transparent adaptation tracking.
methodology: Technical specification proposing a platform for version control and adaptation management of mental health outcome measures
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how data provenance and version control principles apply to web-scraped training corpora or the degradation of measurement quality in large-scale data pipelines.

key_claim: The CARE principles framework reveals hidden costs of archaeological data ecosystems borne by Indigenous communities and repositions Indigenous peoples as active stewards of their data.
methodology: Conceptual analysis applying CARE (Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics) principles to archaeological data governance
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how Indigenous data governance principles might apply to the inclusion or exclusion of culturally specific behavioral data in web-scraped training corpora.
theoretical_framework: CARE Principles (Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics) for Indigenous data governance

key_claim: NHS Digital datasets can be evaluated for data integrity through systematic documentation of data lifecycles, enabling their reliable use in clinical trials.
methodology: Data lifecycle mapping of NHS Digital datasets assessing accuracy, reliability, completeness, and contemporaneity of data flowing from healthcare providers to trial teams
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether healthcare system data integrity frameworks could serve as models for verifying provenance and quality of web-scraped training data.

key_claim: Properly filtered and deduplicated web data alone can produce LLMs that significantly outperform models trained on curated high-quality corpora.
methodology: Macrodata Refinement (MDR) pipeline applying stringent URL filtering, document-level deduplication, and line-level heuristic filtering to CommonCrawl, training 1B and 7B parameter Falcon models on 350B tokens
limitation: RefinedWeb toxicity is comparable to The Pile but social biases and harmfulness beyond rudeness are not evaluated; the potential of multi-epoch training as an alternative to unique token scaling is not fully explored.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether aggressive filtering and deduplication of web data causes tail collapse or loss of diverse, low-frequency human behavioral signals critical for model generalization.

key_claim: FineWeb, a 15-trillion token dataset from 96 Common Crawl snapshots, produces better-performing LLMs than other open pretraining datasets through systematic deduplication and filtering.
methodology: Systematic ablation of dataset curation choices (text extraction, deduplication, filtering) using paired training runs of identically-architected models on equal token counts, evaluated on downstream benchmarks
limitation: Code content is likely not prevalent due to filtering steps; Wikipedia content formatting may be suboptimal compared to specialized curated sources.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether iterative web scraping across Common Crawl snapshots leads to information degradation or declining content diversity over time.

key_claim: C4 contains significant unexpected content including patents, machine-generated text, benchmark data contamination, and social biases, while blocklist filtering disproportionately excludes minority voices.
methodology: Corpus analysis documenting metadata provenance, content inspection for machine-generated text and biases, and examination of blocklist filtering effects on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus (C4)
limitation: Only some possible issues were examined; personally identifiable information and copyrighted text were not quantified; LangID tools work disproportionately well for English, limiting generalizability to other languages.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantify how the documented quality issues in web-scraped corpora compound over time as models trained on such data generate content that re-enters the web.

key_claim: AI-generated content in recently created Wikipedia pages has markedly increased since GPT-3.5's release, with detectors finding at least 5% of new pages are AI-generated at a 1% false positive threshold.
methodology: Application of GPTZero (proprietary) and Binoculars (open-source) AI detectors to recently created Wikipedia pages, with thresholds calibrated at 1% false positive rate, comparing pre- and post-GPT-3.5 pages
limitation: Proprietary nature and cost of GPTZero limit scale; compute requirements of Binoculars bound study size; only two detectors were used, limiting generalizability.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not measure how AI-generated Wikipedia content, when re-ingested into web-scraped training corpora, contributes to information degradation and diversity loss in downstream models.

key_claim: Privacy policies are becoming longer, harder to read, and more ambiguous over time, while severely underreporting usage of third parties and tracking technologies.
methodology: Longitudinal corpus curation of 1,071,488 privacy policies from the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine spanning over two decades, with automated trend surfacing and analysis of tracking technology disclosure
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the evolving nature of boilerplate legal text in web corpora (like privacy policies) contributes to content homogenization and entropy decline in web-scraped training datasets.

key_claim: Online attention is increasingly concentrated among a small number of dominant domains, with the top 10 domains on Reddit growing from 35% to 60% of all attention between 2006 and 2019.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal analysis of outbound weblinks from Reddit and Twitter (2006–2019) combined with Common Crawl PageRank data and economic performance data to measure attention concentration across online companies.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how increasing concentration of online attention affects the diversity and representativeness of web-scraped training data for AI models.
theoretical_framework: Preferential attachment and increasing returns economics applied to online attention markets

key_claim: Approximately 5% of tokens in C4 and other major AI training corpora have become restricted by robots.txt in less than a year, and nearly 45% carry some form of Terms of Service restrictions.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal audit of consent protocols (robots.txt and Terms of Service) across 14,000 web domains underlying AI training corpora C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma.
limitation: The data ecosystem's inconsistencies and omissions between robots.txt and terms of service pages make it difficult to reliably signal or enforce data use preferences.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how the progressive restriction of high-quality web domains degrades the distributional properties (tail coverage, entropy, diversity) of remaining available training data.

key_claim: Despite GDPR and CCPA, privacy policies show increasing use of location data, implicit data collection, lack of meaningful choice, and growing data sharing with unnamed third parties over 25 years.
methodology: Longitudinal NLP and machine learning analysis of over 50,000 privacy policies (1996–2021) retrieved from the Wayback Machine, using 22 trained classifiers for data practices and attributes.
limitation: Focus on English-language policies only; additional limitations stem from the process of retrieving privacy policies and the methods for evaluating their content.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how evolving privacy restrictions and data collection practices affect the availability and quality of authentic human behavioral data for AI training.

key_claim: Over 25+ years, web content has shifted from news and education-related websites toward streaming media and social networking sites, with major changes in dominant platforms.
methodology: Longitudinal quantitative analysis of the top 100 Alexa websites over 25+ years using archived snapshots from the Internet Archive (Wayback Machine).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how the decline of informational web content and rise of multimedia/social networking platforms affects the diversity and information quality of web-scraped AI training corpora.

key_claim: LLM False Belief Test performance shows nuanced associations with scale and post-training but is not robust to False vs. True and explicit vs. implicit variations.
methodology: Bayesian logistic regression on 17 open-weight LLMs tested on 192 balanced False Belief Test variants, with data leakage analysis and pre-training dynamics tracking via OLMo 2
limitation: Substantial variance across model families due to differences in architecture, training data, and optimization techniques limits generalizability of LLM-generic effects
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether socio-cognitive competence in LLMs degrades as web-scraped training corpora lose diversity of authentic human social interaction data over time.
dataset_or_benchmark: False Belief Test variants (Trott et al., 2023)
theoretical_framework: Theory of Mind (ToM) as assessed via the False Belief Test paradigm

key_claim: Nearly all advanced prompting approaches fail to make LLMs replicate human behavior distributions in the 11-20 money request game, revealing fundamental gaps in strategic reasoning.
methodology: Testing multiple LLMs on the 11-20 money request game with varied prompting strategies, comparing response distributions against human subject data and Nash Equilibrium predictions, analyzing memorization, prompt brittleness, and framing sensitivity
limitation: Authors acknowledge challenges including flawed benchmarks, data contamination, and inconsistent LLM performance that make assessing intelligence capabilities difficult
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether LLM failures to replicate human strategic behavior stem from the absence of embodied, physically-verified social behavioral data in training corpora.
theoretical_framework: Game-theoretic reasoning depth evaluation via Nash Equilibrium benchmarking

key_claim: Finetuning LLMs on individual-level social science experiment responses yields predictions 26% more distributionally aligned with human responses in completely unseen studies compared to base models.
methodology: Supervised finetuning of Qwen2.5-14B and LLaMA-8B on SOCSCI210 dataset of 2.9M individual responses from 210 social science experiments, with GPT-4o-mini generated oracle reasoning traces
limitation: Performance plateaus quickly with more participant data likely due to small model sizes; reliance on GPT-4o-mini for reasoning traces may be suboptimal; training exclusively on SOCSCI210 limits diversity
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the authentic human behavioral data used for finetuning could be replaced by synthetic data or whether web-scraped training data degradation undermines base model social reasoning.

key_claim: LLM behavioral simulations in social science require rigorous methodological standards including model selection justification, replicability protocols, prompt documentation, and output validity checks.
methodology: Methodological checklist and best-practice guidelines synthesis for validating LLM use in social science research
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not evaluate how training data composition or degradation of web-scraped corpora affects the validity of LLM-based behavioral simulations.

key_claim: Base LLMs already exhibit non-trivial pragmatic competence in contrastive pragmatic reasoning tasks, even before instruction tuning and DPO alignment.
methodology: Introduced ALTPRAG dataset grounded in pragmatic alternatives; evaluated LLMs at different training stages using GPT-4.1 as judge with 10-point scoring and pairwise comparison metrics.
limitation: Dataset does not distinguish among pragmatic phenomena types (humor, irony, indirect speech); all evaluated models across training stages are from the same organization (AI2), limiting generalizability; moderate dataset scale constrains coverage.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether pragmatic competence originates from diverse authentic human conversational data versus web-scraped text, or how training data composition affects pragmatic reasoning quality.

key_claim: A language model fine-tuned on Psych-101, a large-scale dataset of human behavioral experiments, can predict and simulate human behavior across diverse cognitive tasks and generalize to unseen participants and novel domains.
methodology: Fine-tuned a state-of-the-art language model on the novel Psych-101 dataset covering human behavioral experiment data; evaluated using negative log-likelihoods averaged over responses with out-of-distribution generalization checks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether authentic behavioral data from naturalistic social settings (as opposed to controlled lab experiments) would provide additional irreplaceable training signals for cognitive modeling.
theoretical_framework: Unified foundation model of human cognition (Centaur)

key_claim: LLMs show systematic deviations from human-like theory of mind behavior that are only revealed through a comprehensive multi-test battery, with GPT-4 matching or exceeding humans on most tests except faux pas understanding.
methodology: Compared GPT-4, GPT-3.5, and LLaMA2-70B against human participants across a comprehensive battery of theory of mind tests (false beliefs, indirect requests, irony, faux pas) with multiple sessions, structural/content variations, and inter-rater coded responses.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether LLM theory of mind deficiencies stem from the lack of authentic human social behavioral data in web-scraped training corpora versus architectural limitations.

key_claim: LLMs exhibit a clear gap in theory of mind reasoning for open-ended questions compared to humans, though incorporating mental states through prompt tuning improves their ToM performance.
methodology: Evaluated Zephyr-7B, Llama2-Chat-13B, and GPT-4 on open-ended ToM reasoning using Reddit ChangeMyView data; assessed via human-based evaluation and NLP quantitative metrics; tested prompt tuning with explicit intention and emotion integration.
limitation: Subjectivity remains a potential factor despite using five different reasoning answers; uncertain whether LLMs can inherently account for mental states without explicit prompt tuning.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the gap in LLM ToM reasoning is attributable to information degradation or diversity loss in web-scraped training data versus inherent architectural limitations.

key_claim: Large pretrained language models achieve only up to 65% accuracy on Social IQA compared to nearly 90% human performance, showing social commonsense reasoning remains challenging for AI.
methodology: Crowdsourced 38,000 multiple-choice questions about social situations with adversarial question-switching to reduce stylistic artifacts; fine-tuned OpenAI-GPT and BERT models on the benchmark.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the gap in social reasoning stems from deficiencies in web-scraped training data versus the absence of grounded social behavioral signals.

key_claim: Out-of-the-box GPT-3 models lag behind humans by over 30% on social intelligence benchmarks and struggle with mental state reasoning compared to factual questions.
methodology: Evaluated GPT-3 variants (Ada, Curie, Davinci) on SocialIQA and ToMI benchmarks via API; compared zero-shot and few-shot performance against human baselines and fine-tuned models.
limitation: Authors acknowledge they only evaluated GPT-3 via API and lacked access to larger models like PaLM or computational resources for open-source alternatives like OPT.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically test whether augmenting training data with physically-verified authentic social behavioral data would improve Theory of Mind capabilities.
theoretical_framework: Pragmatics-based perspective on LLM pretraining limitations for Theory of Mind

key_claim: LLMs show uneven Theory of Mind capabilities, performing well on emotion and desire tasks but struggling with complex multi-state reasoning and moral reasoning in multimodal settings.
methodology: Proposed CoMMET, a multimodal benchmark inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task covering all ATOMS mental states plus moral reasoning; evaluated models including LLaMA-3.2-11B and LLaMA-4-Maverick across multiple conversational turns with feedback.
limitation: Authors note issues with image generation quality including incorrect spatial relationships, and one story turn was incorrectly categorized.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the observed ToM deficits in LLMs are attributable to the lack of authentic human social interaction data in training corpora versus architectural limitations.

key_claim: Training with adversarially hard social reasoning examples via reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback significantly improves LLMs' genuine social reasoning and robustness over models relying on superficial pattern matching.
methodology: Introduced ToMBench-Hard adversarial benchmark; trained Social-R1 model using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback (RLVF) on hard social reasoning examples; conducted stage-wise diagnosis using Social Information Processing (SIP) framework and perturbation analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether incorporating authentic human social behavioral data rather than adversarially curated text-based examples could provide more robust and transferable social reasoning capabilities.

key_claim: Bootstrapping instruction data from a language model's own generations and fine-tuning on it yields a 33% absolute improvement on SUPERNI over the original GPT-3.
methodology: Pipeline that generates instructions, inputs, and outputs from GPT-3, filters invalid or similar samples, then fine-tunes the original model on 52K self-generated instructions.
limitation: Authors acknowledge limitations including potential biases inherited from the base model and the dependency on the quality of self-generated data, noting the work should inspire future research to address these issues.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether self-generated synthetic instruction data suffers from diversity loss or entropy decline compared to authentic human-written data over iterative bootstrapping cycles.

key_claim: A large-scale open-source dataset of human-generated conversations for LLM alignment can be crowdsourced to democratize RLHF research.
methodology: Crowdsourced collection of human-generated assistant conversations with quality guidelines, followed by supervised fine-tuning and RLHF training on the collected data.
limitation: Reward models were trained on rankings of human-generated messages rather than model-generated messages as in InstructGPT, which may explain why RLHF models did not show the same uniform improvement over SFT models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether crowdsourced conversational data exhibits the same diversity and authenticity properties as naturally occurring human social behavioral data versus web-scraped text.

key_claim: Fine-tuning a 65B LLaMA model on only 1,000 carefully curated examples without RLHF produces competitive performance with state-of-the-art models, supporting the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis.
methodology: Standard supervised fine-tuning of LLaMA-65B on 1,000 manually curated prompt-response pairs, evaluated via human preference comparisons against GPT-4, DaVinci003, and Alpaca.
limitation: The mental effort to construct high-quality examples is significant and difficult to scale, and LIMA is not as robust as product-grade models—adversarial prompts or unlucky decoding can produce weak responses.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality of pretraining data (e.g., web-scraped vs. physically-verified human behavioral data) modulates the effectiveness of minimal alignment fine-tuning.

key_claim: Evol-Instruct, which uses LLMs to iteratively rewrite instructions into more complex versions, produces training data that enables WizardLM to outperform Alpaca and Vicuna on multiple benchmarks.
methodology: Proposed Evol-Instruct pipeline that evolves seed instructions through in-depth and in-breadth rewriting using an LLM, filters low-quality outputs, then fine-tunes LLaMA on the evolved instruction data.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether LLM-generated evolutionary instruction data converges toward homogeneous patterns over iterations, potentially reducing diversity compared to authentic human-generated instructions.

key_claim: With only 10% of selectively chosen instruction tuning data guided by the Instruction-Following Difficulty metric, models exceed performance of models trained on the full dataset.
methodology: Proposed Instruction-Following Difficulty (IFD) metric to score training samples, trained pre-experienced models on LLaMA-2, then selected cherry samples from Alpaca and WizardLM datasets for fine-tuning
limitation: The pre-experienced model training phase is inconvenient and makes the method hard to directly deploy in real-world scenarios.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether IFD-based selection can detect or mitigate degraded or low-diversity patterns in web-scraped training corpora.

key_claim: A hybrid fine-tuning approach combining real-world transcribed interactions with synthetic data consistently outperforms models fine-tuned with real data alone in therapeutic dialogue generation.
methodology: Fine-tuned three LLM variants (base, real-data-only, hybrid) on a dataset combining transcribed real therapeutic interactions with synthetic persona-driven sessions; evaluated via GPT-4o-simulated conversations scored for empathy and relevance across 50 therapeutic scenarios
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze whether synthetic data introduces distributional biases or entropy loss compared to authentic human behavioral data in therapeutic settings.

key_claim: LLM-generated synthetic empathetic data, filtered through quality and diversity selection, enables models to outperform all previous models including those trained on human-labeled empathetic data.
methodology: Developed Synth-Empathy pipeline using LLM-based data generation with quality scoring and diversity selection modules, then fine-tuned models on curated synthetic empathetic data
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether synthetic empathy data captures the full range of authentic human emotional expression or whether it narrows the distribution of empathetic responses compared to real human-labeled data.

key_claim: Human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues yields higher perceived quality data and improves fine-tuned dialogical model outputs, though effects vary with model size.
methodology: Generated Italian dialogues via ChatGPT, had human annotators post-edit them to create HED-IT corpus (original vs. post-edited), then fine-tuned Pythia models in three sizes and evaluated via automatic and human evaluation
limitation: Resource is only available in Italian; testing was limited to Pythia models; prompting autoregressive models can lead to hallucinations; data was generated by a single LLM (ChatGPT).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether human post-editing restores authentic social behavioral signals lost in machine-generated text or whether it merely corrects surface-level artifacts.

key_claim: Human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues improves both perceived data quality and the quality of fine-tuned dialogical language models.
methodology: Created HED-IT, a corpus of machine-generated Italian dialogues paired with human post-edited counterparts, then fine-tuned Pythia models in three sizes and evaluated via automatic and human evaluation.
limitation: Resource is only in Italian; data generated by prompting ChatGPT may contain hallucinations; testing was limited to only a subset of possible models (Pythia).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether human post-editing can counteract systematic diversity loss or entropy decline in web-scraped training corpora at scale.

key_claim: Scaling diverse, high-quality synthetic instructional conversations (UltraChat) for fine-tuning LLaMA produces a chat model (UltraLLaMA) that outperforms existing open-source models across multiple benchmarks.
methodology: Systematically generated a large-scale multi-turn instructional conversation dataset (UltraChat) using ChatGPT without human queries, drawing on C4 corpus materials, then fine-tuned LLaMA to create UltraLLaMA and evaluated with automatic benchmarks and ChatGPT-based evaluation.
limitation: Evaluation of LLM response quality may have biases; ChatGPT rather than GPT-4 was used for automatic evaluation; UltraChat supports only English.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether fully synthetic instructional data introduces information degradation or diversity loss compared to authentic human conversational data.

key_claim: Conversational data from LLM interactions serves dual roles: internally for fine-tuning and model improvement, and externally for user analytics and behavior prediction.
methodology: Literature review of internal and external applications of LLM conversational data, including supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, and multi-turn dialogue supervised fine-tuning, contextualized within current regulations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether LLM-generated conversational data suffers from entropy decline or tail collapse compared to physically-verified authentic human behavioral data.
